exercises.prompt: Implement add(a, b) so it returns the sum of the two numbers a and b.
hints: A function only hands a value back when you use the return keyword — without it, add gives back undefined and every test fails.
The + operator adds two numbers together: a + b.
The whole body is one line: return a + b.

exercises.prompt: Implement max(a, b) so it returns the larger of the two numbers. If they are equal, return that shared value.
hints: Compare the two values with the greater-than operator: a > b.
The ternary a > b ? a : b returns a when a is bigger and b otherwise — the same as an if/else.
When a and b are equal, a > b is false, so returning b (or a) gives the right shared value.

exercises.prompt: Implement fizzbuzz(n). Return 'Fizz' if n is divisible by 3, 'Buzz' if divisible by 5, 'FizzBuzz' if divisible by both, and otherwise the number itself as a string — fizzbuzz(7) returns '7'.
hints: The remainder operator % tells you divisibility: n % 3 === 0 is true exactly when n is a multiple of 3.
Check the both-divisible case first — n % 15 === 0 — otherwise 15 would match 'Fizz' and stop before reaching 'FizzBuzz'.
For the fall-through case, convert the number to text with String(n) so you return '7', not the number 7.
